posts.caption:
Wearing @isseymiyakeofficial, @solangeknowles – our annniversary issue cover star – arrived last night at a black-tie gala marking one hundred years of the @schomburgcenter for Research in Black Culture, where she was awarded a Schomburg Medal of Excellence. ⁠
⁠
A cornerstone of @nypl, the centre is dedicated to collecting, preserving and making accessible materials that document the global Black experience, from rare archives and manuscripts to exhibitions, research and public programmes. It’s a mission that mirrors Knowles’ own work: through @saintheron, her multidisciplinary platform and archive, she has spent the past decade building spaces for Black cultural memory. ⁠
⁠
“A lot of the questions I’ve been asking myself lately surround the urgency of Black women’s history. It almost feels like survival,” Solange told AnOther Magazine in our latest issue. Revisit the interview at the link in bio 📲⁠
⁠
📸 by @andymartinezx1
posts.alt_text: Photo shared by AnOther Magazine on May 02, 2026 tagging @solangeknowles, and @isseymiyakeofficial. May be an image of overcoat and text.

posts.caption:
@lottaantonssonstudio was introduced to collage as a child in the 1960s, scouring magazines her mother brought home from the hospital, where she worked nights as a nurse.⁠
⁠
In I Am Everything at @fotografiska.stockholm, Antonsson takes those formative years and her personal archive of vintage images, collected between 1968 and 1978, as her starting point. Rather than reduced to photogenic objects, Antonsson’s subjects – usually young, “nude” and in repose – accrue layers. Gemstones, crystals and seashells are applied to their eyes, mouths and breasts in a bid to divert or return the male gaze.⁠
⁠
“I want to lure you in with beauty, and then you find something that is compulsive, or confronting or scary,” Antonsson tells @rosehighamstainton. Read the interview at the link in bio 📲⁠
⁠
📸 I Am Everything by Lotta Antonsson⁠
1. Masque Other Ache⁠
2. Sans Titre (Hommage a Lategan)⁠
3-4. Jeanne, Collage med snäcka, 2021⁠
5. Ocean, 2021⁠
7. Sister, 2024⁠
8. Ali, 2024⁠
9. Her Kind, självporträtt

posts.caption:
In a new exhibition at @donald_ryan_gallery in New York, photographer @devclaro shares his latest body of work, Crushing, a series of carefully staged photos that depict his version of America, shot in the city and its surrounding outer boroughs – in parking lots, on street corners, surrounded by municipal buildings and civic bridges.⁠
⁠
Claro’s outer borough New York is neither utopian or dystopian. It’s not romanticised and it’s not documentarian. In many ways it’s not even America. “Even if there’s still conflict, poverty, racism, and ritual too, I can’t claim to examine the rest of the country, because my America is a micro-pocket,” he tells @rose_dodd. ⁠
⁠
Read the interview at the link in bio 📲⁠
⁠
📸 Devlin Claro: Crushing. Photography by @devclaro. Courtesy of the artist and Donald Ryan Gallery⁠
1. Blades of Glory⁠
2. Blushing⁠
3. The Plaza⁠
5. Different day⁠
6. Ali 2000 (2026)⁠
8-9. YouTube's Baby (2026)

posts.caption:
Happy birthday @kirstendunst, who turns 44 years old today. ⁠
⁠
The actress has made a very public journey from dimpled child walking the red carpet in knee-high socks and jellies via the American sweetheart whose sunny exterior was undercut by choppier waters, to today’s versatile, virtuoso performer with a streak of auteur-driven, break-your-heart performances that hinge on the cartography of her face. “I grew up as I was doing this – I didn’t know what I liked in film as a kid,” she told AnOther. “I had to figure out my own taste, learn all my lessons along the way.” ⁠
⁠
Revisit the interview at the link in bio 📲 ⁠
⁠

posts.caption:
History has a peculiar hold over certain places, forever dangling the memory of an earlier, often better time over us. Ibiza is one such place, where different generations will always say theirs was the last to experience it before its supposed ruin. ⁠
⁠
What we can say for certain is that 50 years ago, when German photographer Walter Rudolph went there with his camera in tow, the island – large parts of it, at least – would have been unrecognisable compared to today.⁠
⁠
By the mid-1970s, the kernels of nightlife culture were already there (Pacha opened in 1973) and tourism was on the rise, but Ibiza was still an outpost for bohemians, a few years away from becoming the land of superclubs. “It was just in that sweet spot between still being in the past and being popular,” says @emmasalahi, owner of Ibiza’s @agonyecstasygallery, which is publishing a new book of Rudolph’s photographs that document this juncture in the island’s history. ⁠
⁠
@himeganwilliams speaks with Salahi about Walter Rudolph’s sun-drenched photographs at the link in bio 📲⁠
⁠
📸 #WalterRudolph: Ibiza 1976 © United Archives, Walter Rudolph, Courtesy of Agony + Ecstasy

posts.caption:
Playing with fishing nets and plastic shells under the banner of brand nostalgia feels disingenuous to me. Even if the collection’s materials are eco-friendly (one can hope), they casually ignore a grim fact: these nets make up around 10% of the plastic waste in our oceans, claiming countless lives—maybe even those of the mermaids after they’ve left the runway for the sea.
In „Entangled“, our artwear exhibition, we tackled the same materials, but with a sharper lens. It’s not as easy to digest, but it’s more authentic.
Of course, the Chanel collection exudes effortless elegance. Who wouldn’t prefer to associate fishing nets with idyllic beach memories in Biarritz rather than the pollution drifting at sea? We see the charm. But we also urge fashion powerhouses to use their platform to confront the issue. To move beyond symbolism and take action against destruction. That doesn’t mean compromising on style—just embracing a little more awareness.

Have you seen Dior’s latest campaign? It carries a particular sense of familiarity — not in its clothing or styling alone, but in the world it sits in.
A visual connection recently shared by @meelzonart led us to revisit the imagery of Paris café society, where artists captured the emergence of modern urban life in its most social forms — cafés, concert halls, and bustling boulevards filled with observation and display.
When placed alongside works by Manet, Repin, and Degas, the campaign begins to resonate in unexpected ways.
Next-Level Fashion. Daily.

posts.caption:
Agent Provocateur 14th Of February 2005 😮‍💨 ❤️ For their 10th Anniversary Party
Held in London on Valentine’s Day, the event blurred the line between fashion show and performance.
Models moved through dimly lit spaces in corsets, latex, and stockings, turning lingerie into something staged and public rather than private.
Founded by Joseph Corré and Serena Rees, the brand carried a direct link to Vivienne Westwood, who was in attendance that night, bringing punk lineage into a space defined by sexuality and spectacle.
The focus stayed on atmosphere: voyeurism, control, and performance. It reflected an early 2000s moment where fashion leaned into excess and hyper-visibility.
More than a celebration, it reinforced Agent Provocateur as a cultural force using lingerie not just as clothing, but as a statement on identity and exposure.

posts.caption:
At Coachella 2017, Rihanna iconicly wore a full crystal-encrusted mesh bodysuit and matching hood directly from Alessandro Michele’s Gucci Fall/Winter 2017 runway show.
She styled this “human disco ball” look with a “Common Sense is Not Common” slogan tank top, denim cutoffs, and platform sneakers, creating one of the festival’s most memorable moments
The look resulted in a 203% surge in searches for Gucci logo T-shirts, according to fashionista.com.
Via @bella.feoli

posts.caption:
Between walking runways and fronting campaigns internationally to her silver screen debut in ‘Anyone but You’, @charleefraser is a multi-talented woman. But among Fraser’s manifold skills is one talent that she has, until now, kept on the quiet. “I love roller skating,” she shares. “I’m constantly thinking with the work that I do and [my] schedule. I’m a major organiser – such a Capricorn,” she laughs. “[With] my skates on, the only thing I can think about is what I’m doing. And it just really grounds me, brings me back into that present.”
Through the link in bio, see Charlee Fraser photographed at sunrise on the Northern Beaches of Sydney for BAZAAR.
Charlee Fraser wears @ralphlauren Collection in the April 2026 issue of Harper’s BAZAAR Australia/New Zealand.

posts.caption:
“Oh, man, little baby Charlee,” @charleefraser chuckles as she reflects on her career beginnings. “It was really interesting because I wasn’t looking to model at the time – the opportunity kind of presented itself,” she says of her beginning. By 2016, Fraser had been modelling locally for three years, with early gigs including runway appearances for David Jones, Carla Zampatti and Bec + Bridge. “My agency was like, ‘I think it’s time for you to go overseas. You’ve done everything here. Go spread the wings’,” she says. “At the time, I really felt ready for that.”
Continue reading Charlee Fraser’s interview with BAZAAR at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
It's strange: The fashion industry is responsible for more than a third of the plastic waste in the ocean. And the trend is rising. And yes, it's primarily the fast-fashion companies, not the luxury brands, but shouldn't the major players, who receive as much attention as Chanel, take a stand instead of clinging to a folkloric, nostalgic view of the ocean that's no longer understandable given the circumstances? Is it really necessary to send plastic fishing nets and shells down the runway without at least a hint of outrage?
Or is it part of such a brand's image to communicate a "don't look up" mentality? I wish they would take more responsibility.

posts.caption:
For four decades, Dries Van Noten defined a singular path in global fashion with a universe rooted in intellectual rigour, exquisite craftsmanship and independence. When he stepped back from his eponymous brand last year, it wasn’t a retreat into a quiet retirement. Instead, Van Noten has embarked on a profound transition — moving from the relentless, dictated rhythm of fashion to a new life as a custodian of culture in Venice.
Van Noten has established a new foundation (@fondazionedriesvannoten) at the Palazzo Pisani Moretta, a space dedicated to the beauty of craftsmanship and the belief that in a world marked by global uncertainty, the act of making something beautiful is the ultimate form of protest.
In this special episode of The BoF Podcast, our editor-at-large Tim Blanks (@timblanks) speaks to Dries Van Noten about this remarkable transition to becoming a custodian of beauty.
🎙️ Listen now #linkinbio #TheBoFPodcast
📷 Fe Pinheiro
posts.alt_text: Photo by The Business of Fashion on May 02, 2026. May be an image of one or more people, magazine, poster and text that says 'BOF The BoF Podcast Podcast Inside Dries Van Noten's Venice Manifesto'.

posts.caption:
As the initial results of the creative change sweeping the fashion industry have begun to trickle in, Matthieu Blazy’s Chanel revamp cemented its position at the front of the pack this week, topping the revamped Lyst Index, a measure of brand heat.
Since its October reveal, the style reboot at the French fashion giant has stood out amid the sector-wide reset, dominating discussions online and sparking shopping frenzies in boutiques.
BoF's Mimosa Spencer explores whether the popularity of Blazy’s revamp raises questions about brand heat — and the risk of a rapid cool-down if “Blazymania” goes unmanaged. Read more #linkinbio
📷 @chanelofficial

But could Blazymania go too far?'.

posts.caption:
Last night, guests descended on House of Koko in London for an intimate community dinner co-hosted by BoF and luxury fragrance house @AmouageOfficial. Convening a curated group of leading voices across fashion, art and culture, the evening fostered a nuanced exploration of luxury’s evolving language — and the increasing role of sensorial experiences in shaping memory, identity and desire.
Set within the private members’ spaces, the dinner gathered founders, creative directors and cultural leaders — from @harris_reed and @adotthegreatt to @tishweinstock, @robertwun and @biancasaunders — for dynamic, cross-sector dialogue and open creative exchange.
Against a backdrop of shifting consumer expectations and a renewed focus on emotional storytelling, conversations examined how to cultivate deeper resonance beyond products, and what it means to create enduring value.

posts.caption:
This week in fashion: Adidas’ London Marathon win; Blazymania cruises into Biarritz — plus more industry moves that hit, and missed, the mark.
• @Adidas scored a big PR win at the London Marathon when two runners in its new Pro Evo 3 supershoe made history by completing the race in under two hours, finishing first and second while the top runner in Nike came in third, one day before the shoe’s North American release.
• Matthieu Blazy's first cruise show for @ChanelOfficial fuelled growing 'Blazymania' as the French couture giant cemented the success of its creative revamp.
• @Vinted hit an €8 billion valuation in a secondary share sale, up 60 percent from the €5 billion valuation it achieved in a similar 2024 transaction, as resale continues to grow.
• @Prada Group reported modest growth, missing analyst estimates as momentum at once-explosive @MiuMiu fell back down to earth.
• @SwanBeauty_US’ skin-analysing mirror was all over social media, thanks to its shrewd sponsorship of influencer Brigette Pheloung's (@acquired.style) lavish bachelorette party.
• India’s Reliance Retail is aiming to scale Priyanka Chopra Jonas' (@PriyankaChopra) hair-care brand @AnomalyHaircare after acquiring it for an undisclosed sum.
• “The Devil Wears Prada 2” accurately chronicles the decline of traditional fashion media — but lacks the punch of the original.
This Week in Fashion is BoF’s weekly news briefing, available exclusively to BoF Professional members. Become a BoF Professional member to access the full analysis #linkinbio

posts.caption:
I want to be Dries Van Noten when I grow up. After four decades as fashion’s favourite icon of independence, he has embarked on an enviable next act by establishing his own art foundation in a 15th century palazzo on the Grand Canal in Venice.
The @FondazioneDriesVanNoten opened its first exhibition last week. He’s called it “The Only True Protest Is Beauty” and he mounts a convincing case for that proposal across more than 200 pieces of art, craft and fashion, all displayed against the imposing backdrop of the palazzo itself with ceilings that stretch to 6.5-metres high.
The exhibition’s title is a quote by songwriter Phil Ochs, a contemporary of Bob Dylan’s in the Sixties: “In such an ugly time, the only true protest is beauty.” No coincidence obviously, although Van Noten thought when he originally chose Och’s words 18 months ago, the state of global affairs would surely have improved by the time the show opened. “I think everybody knows it’s an ugly time, and I’m a positive person, I don’t want to say, ‘Poor us, look where we are, the world is so bad.’ No, we have to look forward.”
In conversation with @TimBlanks, Dries Van Noten reflects on why “retirement” was never part of the plan, and how Venice is shaping his next chapter — from fashion to foundation, from Antwerp to La Serenissima, his story is far from finished.
Read more #linkinbio
📷 @camillaglorioso, @demayda
posts.alt_text: Photo shared by The Business of Fashion on May 01, 2026 tagging @timblanks. May be an image of ‎magazine, poster and ‎text that says '‎BOF ان Dries Van Noten's Next Act There is no such thing as retirement for the Belgian designer. With his new Venice foundation, he's blossoming professionally and personally under the influence of La Serenissima. BY TIM BLANKS‎'‎‎.

posts.caption:
It’s been a decade since Melissa Morris founded her leather goods startup @Metier, best known for selling £3,650 ($4,900) Private Eye handbags and suede weekenders out of its original store on South Audley Street in Mayfair.
Its discreet branding — an antidote to the logo-driven designs that dominated luxury in the 2010s — and a design ethos focused on functionality and classic style have helped power word-of-mouth momentum for the label, alongside fashion editorials and placements in film and television programmes like “Succession.”
While Métier is closely associated with a cohort of London-based fashion editors and businesswomen who rallied around the brand’s pragmatic, androgynous angle on style (and whose attendance has made the brand’s annual dinner at River Café a fixture of the city’s holiday circuit), its business is increasingly global. International customers account for two-thirds of sales at the Mayfair store.
With profitability now within reach, Métier is raising fresh capital to fuel its next chapter Morris and CEO Sofia Fagring tell BoF’s Robert Williams (@robert.williams.writes). Read more #linkinbio
📷 Courtesy @metier

posts.caption:
It's the world's most prestigious international art exhibition. But when the @labiennale opens next week, it will do so amid a series of crises. Its five-person jury for its illustrious awards program abruptly resigned on Thursday, and it will not receive its customary blessing from Italy's minister of culture Alessandro Giuili.
Now in its 61st edition, the Biennale is a massive, global presentation of contemporary art from 99 nations, exhibited largely in permanent national pavilions dotted around Venice's Giardini della Biennale. The culture minister's absence from the official opening ceremony will be a notable first in the history of the Biennale, which opens May 9.
It was unclear why the jury's members resigned, and following the Biennale's announcement, a spokesperson declined to comment further.
Swipe to see how conflicts in multiple parts of the world are fueling controversy at the Biennale and read the full story at link in bio.
📸: Vincenzo Pinto/AFP/Getty Images | Marco Longari/AFP/Getty Images | Luc Castel/Getty Images

posts.caption:
When "The Devil Wears Prada" was released in 2006, critics adored how it spikily satirized the sudsy but serious business of fashion magazine publishing.
But for fashion insiders, it was another story. Not only did Anna Wintour, upon whom Meryl Streep's character, Miranda Priestly is based, ignore the 2003 book's release, but she glided above the film's existence while nonetheless benefitting from its chilly portrait. (She eventually attended a screening — wearing Prada.)
The clothes, though, were a particular pain point for the industry.
"The @chanelofficial boots that Andy wore, we all thought — this is a very English phrase — gopping error!" @therealplumsykes, a longtime Vogue contributing editor said, referring to the over-the-knee shoes that Andy dons post-makeover.
But in the two decades between "The Devil Wears Prada" and its sequel, fashion editors have come to look fondly on director David Frankel's funhouse vision.
Now, as the fashion world races to embrace the film that it once attempted to take down a peg, it finds itself indulging a vision of fashion that may not jibe with what its own power players deem in style.
The dominance of red carpet and social media, where nostalgia for earlier eras rules, means that fewer people today are learning about fashion from carefully styled magazine spreads that presented runway collections as news and inspiration. "Back then," said Sykes, "if it's already been seen, it was over with a capital O, and it's not a trend unless it's the next thing. With Anna, it was always, What's the news?"
Rather than pushing forward an agenda of newness, the industry has eagerly hopped onto the bandwagon of what was once. The question is: who will tell us what to wear next?
📸 : TheStewartofNY/Getty Images/Barry Wetcher/20th Century Fox/Shutterstock/Macall Polay/20th Century Studios
posts.alt_text: Photo shared by CNN Style on May 01, 2026 tagging @annehathaway, and @stanleytucci. May be an image of dress and text that says '公 DOES FASHION REALLY DRESS LIKE 'ΤΗΕ DEVIL WEARS PRADA'?'.

posts.caption:
Japanese multi-hyphenate creative @nigo famously established the blueprint for modern streetwear with his loud, graphic-heavy, scarcity-driven designs.
Yet, extraordinarily, an exhibition at London's @designmuseum, opening on Friday, is the first to be staged on the designer, who is also a DJ, collector and entrepreneur, outside of his native Japan.
His influence is extensive, with creative tastemakers and collaborators including @pharrell, @ye, @mrkimjones, and the late @virgilabloh, having previously credited Nigo for his impact on their work and fashion more widely. "There wouldn't be @joopiterofficial if it weren't for Nigo," said Williams in 2024, referring to his online auction house selling rare luxury items, art and memorabilia.
Some attentive fans, however, might question why the exhibition is named "From Japan with Love." In a rare interview with CNN, Nigo reflects on the objects displayed in the exhibition and why — after a prolific career influenced by American culture — he feels closer to his home country than ever before.
📸 : Shane Anthony Sinclair/Getty Images/From Elliot James Kennedy/Luke Hayes

posts.caption:
At the Chanel Cruise show in Biarritz, France on Tuesday there was one item that stood out on the runway of models in modern-day beachwear: A pair of 'sandals' that encouraged all five toes to spread free.
The shoes featured a leather heel fastened onto the wearer's foot via two tied straps… and that's all.
Since taking over the creative direction of Chanel in 2025, @matthieu_blazy has had a lot of fun reimagining Chanel's classic cap-toe shoes — from contrasting crescent moons in pony skin and turquoise leather to blocky squared varieties. But perhaps this twist on the toe is his most daring yet.
What do you think of the latest pair from @chanelofficial?
📸 : Photo by Stephane Cardinale - Corbis/Corbis via Getty Images/Marc Piasecki/Dominique Maitre/WWD via Getty Images

posts.caption:
The 80s remain one of fashion’s most recognizable references, not because of nostalgia alone, but because the decade understood impact. In „Collections“ for Vogue Japan March 2018, Luigi & Iango bring that energy into a contemporary context: sharp shoulders, rich textures, bold styling and a sense of glamour that feels confident.
The editorial plays with classic 80s codes without making them look like costume. Anna Dello Russo’s styling mixes structure, shine, lace, leather and exaggerated silhouettes in a way that feels controlled and editorial. The result is dramatic, but never messy — a reminder that excess can still be precise.
Hair and makeup complete the mood with volume, definition and attitude, while the models carry the clothes with the kind of presence the era demands. It feels powerful, visual and slightly theatrical, but still refined. This is why the 80s continue to influence fashion: they gave designers permission to be bold, and editorials like this show how modern that boldness can still feel.
Editorial: Collections
Magazine: Vogue Japan, March 2018
Photographers: Luigi & Iango
Stylist: Anna Dello Russo
Models: Kiki Willems, Lexi Boling, Luna Bijl, Paul Hameline, Selena Forrest, Zhengyang Zhang
Hair: Luigi Murenu
Makeup: Yumi Lee
Publisher: Vogue Japan

posts.caption:
Absent Findings is one of those young fashion brands that seems to exist just outside the usual noise. It has shown in Milan and Dubai Fashionweek, appeared in publications like Vogue Italia, and built real industry credibility. Still, it has not fully crossed into wider fashion awareness.
That is what makes it interesting.
Founded by Shivin Singh, the Dubai-based design studio works with memory, craft, and reconstruction. Its clothes combine South Asian textile references, European tailoring, vintage saris, surplus fabrics, and hand-finished details. The result feels considered, slightly raw, and personal rather than loud or trend-driven.
The brand’s world is built through wrapped tops, loose trousers, sculptural bags, layered fabrics, muted desert tones, and garments that feel lived-in. In its imagery, models stand in open landscapes, quiet architecture, and desert spaces. The clothes are not styled for obvious impact; they sit naturally inside the atmosphere.
On paper, Absent Findings is already more established than many people might assume. It has presented during Milan Fashion Week, shown at Dubai Fashion Week, and appeared at Lineapelle Designers Edition and Fondazione Sozzani. Press from Vogue, Vogue Italia, Vanity Fair, and other titles adds to that credibility.
What makes the brand stand out is that it still feels understated. The references are visible, but not over-explained. South Asian craft and textile memory are present without feeling decorative. European tailoring is there too, but the clothes remain soft, personal, and slightly offbeat.
Even with the social proof, Absent Findings still feels like something you discover rather than something being pushed at you. In a fashion landscape where young labels often become overexposed quickly, this one seems to be building slowly and with intention.
Maybe that is why it is worth paying attention to now.
Credits :
@absentfindings
@shi.vin
@laura.puscasuu
posts.alt_text: Photo by CULT MAGAZINEⓇ on May 01, 2026. May be a graphic of one or more people, poster, magazine and text that says 'Why is Nobody Talking about this Brand? CULT®'.

posts.caption:
Even though we all already know Lady Diana’s style was amazing, it still deserves to be talked about.
Her style was influential because it felt both royal and real. She could wear a glamorous gown with diamonds one day, then a sweatshirt, biker shorts, and sneakers the next, and somehow both looks became iconic. Diana understood fashion as a form of communication. Her outfits showed confidence, softness, rebellion, elegance, and freedom,  sometimes all at once.
What made her style so cool was that it never felt stuck in one image. She evolved from polished princess looks to bolder, more independent fashion choices, and people followed that transformation closely. The oversized blazers, statement collars, sleek evening dresses, pearls, casual sportswear, and that unforgettable “revenge dress” all became part of her visual legacy.
Lady Diana’s influence is still everywhere today. Celebrities, designers, and fashion lovers continue to reference her looks because they feel timeless but also effortless. She proved that style is not just about looking perfect, it is about owning who you are, even when the whole world is watching.
posts.alt_text: Photo by CULT MAGAZINEⓇ on May 01, 2026. May be an image of one or more people, bangs, blonde hair, people smoking, magazine, poster and text that says 'Forever Style Icon, Lady Di. CULT'.

posts.caption:
The Versace Spring/Summer 2002 campaign captures the unapologetic glamour and sensual excess that defined early-2000s luxury fashion. Photographed by legendary fashion photographer Steven Meisel, the campaign radiates cinematic drama and polished decadence, transforming each image into a study of power, seduction, and high-octane elegance.
Set against sleek, minimal backdrops that amplify the intensity of the styling, the campaign juxtaposes sculptural silhouettes with overt sensuality. The imagery balances sharp eroticism with refined sophistication, creating a distinctly Versace world—one built on confidence, allure, and visual impact. The styling embraces the house’s signature codes: body-conscious tailoring, metallic accents, daring cuts, and hyper-glamorous textures.
The chemistry between the models enhances the narrative, giving the campaign a charged, provocative energy. Every frame feels deliberate and polished, yet emotionally magnetic—an embodiment of the bold and fearless spirit of early-2000s Versace. Through Meisel’s immaculate visual language, the campaign becomes more than fashion imagery; it is a statement of excess, attitude, and timeless glamour.
Creative Director / Designer — Donatella Versace
Photography — Steven Meisel
Styling / Fashion Editor — Lori Goldstein
Hair — Garren
Makeup — Pat McGrath
Models — Sharon Ganish, Sebastien Andrieu, Ana Christina Oliveira

posts.caption:
In the 2000s, Chanel created some of the most iconic and luxurious advertising campaigns in fashion history. The brand focused on elegance, glamour, and cinematic storytelling. One of the most famous campaigns was the 2004 Chanel No. 5 advertisement starring Nicole Kidman and directed by Baz Luhrmann. It was produced like a short Hollywood film, featuring romance, mystery, and high fashion. The campaign showed Chanel not just as a perfume brand, but as a symbol of fantasy, sophistication, and timeless beauty.
Throughout the decade, Chanel’s campaigns often featured famous actresses, luxurious couture, and artistic photography. The visual style was elegant, dramatic, and instantly recognizable. Chanel successfully combined classic French luxury with modern celebrity culture, making its campaigns unforgettable and highly influential in the fashion world.

posts.caption:
Is fashion art?⁠
⁠
It’s an age-old question that’s haunted the industry for decades. If you’re listening to Andre Leon Talley backstage at John Galliano SS94, the answer is a resounding NO. “Fashion is hard work! Gritty. It’s not glamorous,” he famously told reporters. ⁠
⁠
But if you’re listening to the dress code at this year’s Met Gala – sure it is! While The Met’s upcoming exhibition Costume Art finally gives fashion pride of place alongside other iconic works in the archive, the extremely broad style directive for the gala – ‘Fashion is Art’ – could be interpreted in a number of different ways. ⁠
⁠
From @michaelastark to @commedesgarcons – and a classic @alexandermcqueen showpiece, swipe ➡️ for  the looks we’re manifesting on the Met steps⁠
⁠
Tap the link in bio to see more 🔗⁠

posts.caption:
Why do two emeralds that look similar sell at completely different prices?
Emerald pricing is shaped by origin, colour, clarity, and perception. While Colombian, Zambian, and Brazilian emeralds each occupy distinct positions in the market, retail behaviour tells a more nuanced story.
In many cases, customers prioritise visual appeal and price over origin — especially in mid and entry-level segments. For retailers, understanding this difference is key to building a balanced and profitable emerald strategy.
Save this for your gemstone knowledge.
And tell us what your customers actually prioritise.
#DiamondWorld #GemSphere #EmeraldJewellery #MayBirthstone #JewelleryRetail

posts.caption:
Condé Nast recently announced layoffs following a substantial reduction in force just last year. In February, The Washington Post’s staff was all but gutted by Jeff Bezos. The BBC, CBS, and Politico have undergone similar cuts as well. This epidemic of slashed newsrooms is precisely the issue ‘The Devil Wears Prada 2’ set its sights on. For the audience, these issues are all too real.
But, does this long-awaited sequel have the same grit as its 2006 predecessor to really honor the issues it tackles?
Spoiler: It does not.
What it does have is a bogey man called Big Tech. Where DWP1 showed audiences the challenging and ethically dubious business of fashion, DWP2 depicts the tech bro takeover of the fashion world. Nigel quips that Runway is no longer a magazine but is a content machine for people to scroll “while they pee.” And the film’s villains are a pair of devil-may-care Silicon Valley chads a la the Bezos’, Elon Musk, and Bryan Johnson, who believe that the content and operations of ‘Runway’ can be replaced by AI.
What, then, does the film offer as an antidote to a media landscape full of deepfakes and slop? Not only are entire scenes self-referential but they’re literally identical to the first film. Sure, the hallmark DWP outfit montages deliver a few thrills, but they feel awkwardly mimetic and aware of themselves. And, while the lead actors’ charming chemistry holds the film together, they are forced to deliver predictable dialogue in the exact cadence they did twenty years ago. Because of this uncanny duplicity, the film fails to demonstrate the very innovation that it begs for.
As a result, DWP2 feels like a fairytale for a generation of fashion and media professionals who have long grown out of fairytales. It insists that a bootstraps work ethic plus a strong moral compass will win you your wildest dreams, despite all the rich and powerful oppressors you may encounter on the way. But we were told that same tale in 2006… and perhaps that one is better left to nostalgia?
posts.alt_text: Photo by Diet Prada ™ on May 01, 2026. May be an image of eyewear, coat, dinner jacket and text that says 'The Devil Wears Prada 2: Glossy Rehash That Can't Escape Its Own Blueprint Does the sequel hold up with everything that has changed in the last twenty years? Diet PRADA'.

posts.caption:
Why did Matthieu Blazy send newsprint down the Chanel Resort ‘27 runway? Who was he referencing and why? Was it an elbow in the rib to John Galliano, the former Christian Dior creative director who has an outsized claim to newsprint fashion?
Contrary to popular belief, Galliano didn’t show newsprint in his infamous ‘hobo chic’ Christian Dior Spring/Summer 2000 collection. It was the ‘Super Fly Girls’ Fall/Winter 2000 show which featured the ‘The Christian Dior Daily’ textile that we all know and love. Needless to say, that print gained cult-fame after Sarah Jessica Parker wore it as Carrie Bradshaw on ‘Sex and the City’ later that year. And that’s on fashion herstory. No, but seriously, it was huge.
Galliano, though, was not the first to do newsprint. Elsa Schiaparelli’s 1935 newsprint, based on actual newspaper clippings of her accomplishments, is the most well-documented early iteration. In her autobiography ‘Shocking Life,’ she wrote about a seaside experience where she saw fishermen’s wives wearing newspapers “twisted into queer shapes of hats.” Upon returning to Paris, she designed the print, commissioned it, and sold it as “blouses, scarves, hats, and all kinds of bathing nonsense.”
It’s sort of curious to clock newsprint in Chanel’s Resort ‘27 collection, which paid homage to Coco Chanel by taking place in Biarritz, the coastal city where she opened her first couture house. It’s no secret that Chanel famously maintained a rivalry with Schiaparelli throughout her career, calling her “that Italian artist who makes clothes.” A 1940’s Vogue editor, Bettina Ballard, once wrote of a ball where Schiaparelli, who had arrived dressed as a surrealist tree, agreed to dance with Chanel who innocently danced her right into a candelabra, setting her costume ablaze.
So, was Blazy extending a long overdue olive branch from Chanel to Schiaparelli? Or was it a taunt? Is there tea on a Blazy/Roseberry friendship of some sort? Either way, the feud between Chanel and Schiaparelli looked to be the elephant in the room (or, on the beach?) at Chanel Resort ‘27.
And speaking of legendary feuds, is it finally time for Ryan Murphy to take on Schiap and Coco?

posts.caption:
Jennifer Rauchet, Pete Hegseth’s wife, has been accused of wearing Temu to the White House Correspondents’ Dinner.
Rauchet’s husband Hegseth knows how to spend money with reckless abandon. His career is marred with scandal, much of which alleges financial mismanagement. An analysis published in March revealed $93.4 billion dollars was spent by the DoD in September alone. $9 million of that total was reportedly spent on “Alaskan king crab and lobster tails.” Additional purchases include almost $100k for a grand piano and $139k for donuts. Hegseth has also been accused of attempting to personally profit off the war he set in motion, has faced financial mismanagement accusations prior to becoming Trump’s Secretary of War, and paid off an undisclosed sex assault victim with $50k in 2017.
But it appears that despite Hegseth’s ability to throw money around, it has not yet occurred to him to set any money aside for his wife’s wardrobe budget, leaving her to scrape for the cheapest available options from overseas fast fashion retailers.
Indeed, the petal pink evening dress that Rauchet wore to the WHCD perfectly matches with a product listed on both Temu and Shein. Styled with the dress, Rauchet wore Steve Madden ‘Kenley’ rhinestone heeled sandals and she carried a floral appliqué bag which can be found on Amazon.
It doesn’t look like this is the first time Rauchet has done this: In November of last year, she attended a state dinner in a gown that matches a product listing on Shein. And, the pink dress she wore to her husband’s swearing in ceremony also perfectly matches a product made by Shein’s brand Motf. Choosing an inexpensive option to conform to a family budget is one thing, but is it not a little ironic to wear outfits imported from overseas while being married to the ‘America First’ Secretary of Defense? And while attending important events, no less?

posts.caption: In 1985 — way before the MET Gala became too mainstream for y’all — the museum held the party for their ‘Costumes of Royal India’ exhibition. A full oriental bang-fest that would even make a Bhansali film blush. The wall colours were bright enough to give UV damage. A full elephant sculpture on the floor of a room. Diana Vreeland, who organised the event, had the vents sprayed every morning with perfume (Sandalwood by Guerlain). They said fu*ck accuracy and amp up the fantasy. The rules for the gala were different too. Theme dressing di not exist, and the guest list was filled with people with actual influence and money. None of these PR plays and earned media value. But the major difference was how less of a spectacle the affair was. The exhibition had a small spread in the Dec Vogue of that year. The The New York Times reported with a small column in the section B of their newspaper. And the photos? Hardly available, except for Cher in that iconic Bob Mackie. It was for the RICH and by the RICH.

posts.caption:
Babe, wake up! Ranveer has designed his own adidas Originals Superstar. Superstar for THEE superstar!!
A Ranveer Singh for adidas Originals sneaker feels like a long time coming. The RGB colour-way track-sets, the bucket hats, the special collabs, and the plethora of shoes. Ranveer’s wardrobe choice and the Originals identity have merged over time to create a streetwear aesthetic that is instantly recognisable. He has sported a style that no one in his level of fame dares to tread, and the brand has served as a cultural lightning rod, bringing to him the best of global streetwear.
So finally when he gets to be a part of adidas India’s first ever statement shoe drop, it seems like a no-brainer. A MAJOR choice if you may. To collab at this level with Originals is not just an acknowledgment of Ranveer’s love for the brand, but also of his influence. Not influencer, baby, INFLUENTIAL. His ‘superstar’-dom, if you may. As they say, strike the iron while it’s hot, and adidas Originals has clearly clocked it, because if anyone can sell a shoe right now, it’s HIM.

The more compelling story is usually the one unfolding underneath. In the spaces between the headlines. In self-awareness. In the restraint. In the refusal to become too fixed, too quickly. “What I dream of is to do things that scare me a little bit,” she says. Click on the 🔗 in the bio to read the complete interview.

posts.caption:
Lights, camera, @billieeilish.
At just 24 years old, the 10-time Grammy winner and two-time Oscar winner has existed in the public consciousness for a decade now. But this May, a new concert documentary attempts to capture her star power and stage presence in a new light—through the eyes of legendary director James Cameron, no less. Eilish never thought she’d make another documentary after 2021’s ‘Billie Eilish: The World’s a Little Blurry,’ which closely documented her adolescence and the making of her sensational 2019 debut album. “I’m so private about my actual life now,” Eilish tells ELLE, but when the ‘Avatar’ filmmaker came calling, she knew she had to pick up—and co-direct the film alongside him.
In ELLE’s May 2026 cover story, Eilish opens up about collaborating with Cameron, how her personal and professional relationship with her brother @finneas has evolved, and her activist spirit: “I make art and music to reach people, to help people.” Read the full interview at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
The past few years in beauty have been characterized by human intervention in service of an un-human look—overly pillowy lips, puffy faces, complexions buffed to a robot-like shininess, sharply contoured cheeks.
Now, judging by evidence from runway beauty trends and doctors’ offices, we are back in an era of looking merely mortal.
“I used to have 10 to 15 percent reversals. Now it’s up to 20 to 30 percent. Many people are reverting back and trying to make their faces more normalized,” says Daniel Gould, MD, a plastic surgeon in Beverly Hills, who saw the trend starting around five years ago across all kinds of aesthetic procedures. Darren Smith, MD, a plastic surgeon in New York City, has seen a 30 percent uptick in breast reductions over the past 18 months. The words that people are using to describe their desires for their faces go beyond just “natural.” “I want to look like my authentic self” is what facial plastic surgeon Babak Azizzadeh, MD, has heard. Many have begun to course-correct the cosmetic work they once sought after—imperfection is back in.
At the link in bio, ELLE’s @kathleenhou explores how and why many people have begun to course-correct the work they've done on their bodies.

posts.caption:
The first thing you see in 2006's ‘The Devil Wears Prada’ is @annehathaway's Andy Sachs wiping her steamy bathroom mirror and brushing her teeth before her job interview at Runway. But the first thing you hear is “Suddenly I See” by @kttunstall—and the entire opening sequence, like many things from the beloved movie, has become iconic since it first hit screens.
And now, the song is a hit again just in time for the release of ‘The Devil Wears Prada 2’; it's appeared in 1.1 million TikToks to date and earned nearly 117 million streams on Spotify in the past year—with Gen Z being responsible for more than half of them.
“It’s just a thrill,” Tunstall tells ELLE. “All of a sudden, one of your songs gets chosen to be elevated into Hollywood. And not only that, but it’s the opening of now a complete cult classic.”
Two decades on, it’s hard to imagine any other song in that montage—it's still such a perfect fit. At the link in bio, Tunstall and other creatives from the film reveal how it all came to be.
posts.alt_text: Photo by ELLE Magazine on May 01, 2026. May be a meme of poster and text that says 'ELLE EXCLUSIVE KT Tunstall Reveals How 'Suddenly I See Made It Into The Devil Wears Prada'.

posts.caption:
“Hi Shakaila we’re xyz producers and we’d love to make a tv show with you” I’ve had a handful of those conversations over the years and they quickly go from 🤩 to 🫩
I used to feel disheartened until I looked around and saw so many examples of people not waiting for permission and instead deciding to get up and make shit happen! So that’s what I decided to do, turn this decades long dream into a reality! I pulled in every favour I had! Turned my friends into producers and show runners, begged and borrowed and you know what, it turned out pretty damn good if I do say so myself 😅
This black history month especially, I’m inspired by the talented Black British women who have shown me that it’s possible to make a mark on the small screen no matter the limitations!
I absolutely loved makeover shows growing up! But as I got older I saw how they were more brutal than beautiful!
Today, fashion psychology shows us just how deeply our clothes impact our mental health, identity, and confidence.
That’s why I’m so excited to share The Wearapy Show my new makeover series that blends fashion psychology, emotional healing and real style transformations.
We help everyday people reclaim their wardrobes after major life shifts: grief, motherhood, identity struggles, and more.
With a team of experts and no judgment, just science-backed guidance and love.
Episodes 1 and 2 launch 26th October on the Fashion is Psychology YouTube Channel. Subscribe now and follow the journey.
#bhm #wearapy #fashionpsychology #makeover

posts.caption:
What if I told you that “too much” is often exactly what you need
So many of us have been conditioned to shrink ourselves. To tone it down. To be palatable. To choose the safe outfit instead of the one that actually reflects how we want to feel. But your clothes are not just about how you look. They are one of the most powerful forms of non verbal communication you have access to
Before you speak, people have already formed impressions. Research in social psychology shows that we make rapid judgments about confidence, status, and competence within seconds. Your outfit is doing more talking than you think
And it does not stop there. What you wear also changes how you feel. Studies on enclothed cognition show that clothing can influence your mindset, your behaviour, and even your performance. So when you dress in a way that feels bold, expressive, or powerful, you are not just signalling that energy to others, you are creating it within yourself
This is why taking up space through your style matters. Choosing colour. Playing with silhouettes. Wearing the outfit that feels like “a lot”. These are not superficial decisions. They are psychological tools that allow you to step into different versions of yourself
Sometimes your outfit is not just an outfit. It is escapism. It is a way to access confidence when you do not quite feel it yet. It is a way to explore identity, to shift your mood, to process emotion. In fashion psychology, we understand that clothing can support emotional regulation in very real ways
So the next time you think “this is too much”, ask yourself who taught you that
Because that “too much” outfit might be the very thing that helps you feel more like yourself
And maybe the goal is not to take up less space
It is to finally allow yourself to take up more #fashionpsychology

posts.caption: I think this makes a lot of sense. Stress, anxiety and low mood affect our self-worth and identity, which in turn shapes our personal style - they're so intrinsically linked. When you’re not feeling like yourself, it’s incredibly hard to dress in a way that feels authentic. But it becomes a bit of a vicious cycle, because when we do wear clothes we love and that feel true to us, it can have such a positive impact on our mood.

posts.caption:
HUGE NEWS: Good On You has officially rated over 7,000 brands 🎉 This is a major accomplishment, as even more brands—from the small and indie through the large and luxury—are in our Directory.⁠
⁠
What goes into ratings? 🤔 A lot, actually: we have a rigorous, comprehensive system analysing up to 1,000 data points across 100 key issues to arrive at our lowest score (“We Avoid”) to our highest score (“Great”). Ratings are based solely on a brand’s transparent, public reporting that we pair with credible third-party indices, certifications, and standards.⁠
⁠
How do we select brands to rate? Well…with your help 🥹 Many of our ratings come through community requests, shared in comments and messages, which helps us understand which brands to prioritise to meet this moment. Additionally, our retail and ecommerce partners along with brands request ratings too, which is a key means to apply change to the industry from within.⁠
⁠
Another item to celebrate here? Of these rated brands, 1,000 have received our two highest scores, “Good” and “Great”. This is very exciting news, representative of how more brands are doing considering their impact on workers, the planet, and animals. (Even if that also means…6000 brands have a lot more to do to reduce their impact.)⁠
⁠
Here’s to continuing to keep people like you informed while supporting more sustainable brands that are making the difference! Cheers 🥂 Explore our 7,000 ratings at the link too ✨⁠
⁠
#SustainableFashion #FastFashion #ConsciousConsumer #EthicalConsumer #SustainableStyle #ConsciousStyle #GoodOnYou #GoodOnYouApp #EthicalFashion⁠
posts.alt_text: Photo by Good On You: sustainability ratings on January 21, 2026.

posts.caption:
Meet the top rated brands of 2025 🎊⁠
⁠
In the past year, we rated more than 1500 brands and are proud to share the ten that received our highest marks, illustrating a commitment to more sustainable practices that protect people, the planet, and animals 💚⁠
⁠
What’s a theme that unites these brands? 👀 Outside of a commitment to reducing their impact, these brands are all small and independent, a reminder of where to look in the industry for leadership and innovation. If you’re looking for a reminder to support small brands this year, this is it.⁠
⁠
Cheers to all these brands, along with all the brands that were rated “Good” and “Great” in the past year! Here’s to continuing to make a difference and for championing a better approach to what we wear. Find the full list at the link—and share which brands you’re hoping to see rated in 2026 in the comments 💬⁠

posts.caption:
What can be accomplished in ten years?⁠

A decade may seem short but, in reflecting on all the work we’ve done in the past decade, we’re in awe of everything that has transpired—and are grateful for the hard work of the current and former team members along with you.⁠

Whether you’ve shared our posts or have been a partner in this business, the work of creating a more sustainable future isn’t done alone but in partnership with others. These accomplishments are an example of what happens when likeminded people come together in the hopes of a better world 🌍🌎🌏⁠

posts.caption:
This May Day is a great reminder that we aren’t doing enough for garment workers 😭⁠
⁠
Every few months, a story emerges about a luxury fashion house or a fast fashion brand exploiting workers as these brands make billions all as people suffer to make products. This is a tragedy that many in the world unknowingly support, all while enabling the destruction of environments and animal life 💔⁠
⁠
What can you do to make a difference? 🥺 Understand how your favourite brands treat their workers and share what you find, in the hopes of helping others make the right choices: swipe to learn more ⏩⁠
⁠
How does the treatment of workers fit into your relationship to clothes and what you buy? Share in the comments, then visit the link to better understand this issue and how you can help ↑⁠
⁠
#Fashion #SHEINGals #Zara #Sustainability #SustainableFashion⁠

posts.caption:
Don’t take the bait: H&M and Zara are *not* mid-market fashion brands 🚫⁠
⁠
We’re undergoing a shift by fast fashion brands, who are desperate to be seen as luxurious and the makers of “affordable luxury” without doing anything other than luxurywash their business model. This is greenwashing by another name 👎🏽👎🏾👎🏼👎🏿👎🏻⁠
⁠
Why are they doing this? The brands realize their relevance is fading, and they’re hiring the biggest designers while expressing sustainable ideas to seem progressive and cutting edge. Again: don’t take the bait 🤮⁠
⁠
Swipe to learn more about fast fashion’s attempted pivot—and tag a friend to make sure they’re aware too 👀 Find the full story from anxiety.eco at the link ↑⁠
⁠
#ZaraStyle #HMStyle #Fashion #Sustainability #CoolGirlStyle⁠

posts.caption:
One of the loudest concerns when the Devil Wears Prada sequel was announced was that they might butcher the sacred original story. When Runway magazine’s wilful outsider Andy Sachs symbolically tossed her T-Mobile Sidekick into the fountain in Place de la Concorde at the end of the first film, we were all invited to wish for her a better future. One where she could write about things she cared about and eat Jarlsberg toasties at will.
As with anything beloved and nostalgic, sequels sound alarm bells. What if they kill off one of the main characters? What if they turn Miranda Priestly soft? How could the main characters possibly still be in each other’s lives after 20 years?
Fortunately, the writers made a bold (and refreshing) call by introducing us to Andy in her early 40s, single, child-free, career driven and, crucially, happy. They could have easily depicted her as a frazzled mother with a hopeless husband whose burgeoning journalism career fell by the wayside, but they didn’t, and the sequel is all the better for it.
Andy’s love life is little more than a minor a subplot in Devil Wears Prada 2, which for a major blockbuster expected to take in $80 million in its opening weekend, is pretty radical, says @nikpeach at the link in bio.
Writers, we’re sorry for ever doubting you.
posts.alt_text: Photo shared by Grazia UK on May 02, 2026 tagging @nikpeach. May be an image of one or more people, poster, magazine and text that says 'A female protagonist who's single, child free and happy? Andy Sachs' storyline really is groundbreaking GRAZIA'.

posts.caption:
@chloessevigny is the real deal. For three decades, Sevigny has cut a singular path, both on screen and in fashion, building a body of work that feels as unexpected as it does authentically her own. In Bazaar’s May 2026 issue, the actor talks about her next move—“The Five-Star Weekend,” a drama series adapted from the Elin Hilderbrand novel in which she stars alongside Jennifer Garner. It is not the kind of project that one would immediately associate with Sevigny, but that’s precisely why it also makes sense; she’ll never fit neatly into any box. “My agents sent it to me, and I was like, ‘This is too mainstream. My fans are not going to like it,’” Sevigny says, “but I really feel there’s really something for everybody.”
-

posts.caption:
Photographer @yutarosaito presented his solo exhibition “NOT PLASTIC FASHION / LIFE” at @experiment.archive in Shibuya, Tokyo.⁠
⁠
Since 2021, YUTARO had been developing the concept of NOT PLASTIC FASHION. In his first photobook released in November 2021, he highlighted the unique style of elderly men in the streets as a form of fashion directed toward the self, in contrast to the then-prevalent mode featuring extensive use of logos and iconic graphics.⁠
⁠
The project continued to evolve with the new book, 20’s STREET STYLE JOURNAL #1 NOT PLASTIC FASHION (2030), which further explored the fashion of elderly men. Through collages where cityscapes and portraits intersect, the work captured the presence of elderly men while examining the rationality and desire in fashion, the universality of the fashion system, and its possible futures.⁠
⁠
The exhibition featured a series of portraits of elderly men that YUTARO had continuously photographed, alongside a curated selection of old garments he had collected over time, which were also available for purchase.
posts.alt_text: Photo shared by Highsnobiety Style #HSStyle on May 02, 2026 tagging @highsnobietydesign. May be an image of one or more people, people standing, magazine, newspaper, street and text.

Summer heat and World Cup hype are closing in, and @everyone.tokyo⁠ just dropped the uniform for both.⁠
⁠
The Japanese label’s second link-up with adidas taps into that 2006 “teamgeist” energy, serving up an oversized jersey, relaxed track pants, and a throwback waist bag built for match-day chaos or everyday flex. It’s sporty, but not just for sports fans. Think couch yelling, city strolling, or anywhere in between. Ryo Miyoshi also works his quiet magic on the Adizero Adios OG, upgrading the retro runner with luxe, water-repellent leather and suede in slick black or navy.⁠
⁠
Like its name promises, EVERYONE keeps making pieces that actually feel for everyone, blending performance roots with low-key fashion polish. From past sneaker upgrades to silky collabs with A.PRESSE, the label keeps refining the basics until they feel anything but basic.

posts.caption:
@keen's Jasper has been a staple since 2008… climbing-inspired, low-profile and recognizable by its toe-to-tongue lacing and earthy colorways. Now, the brand is revisiting it. The Jasper KM Lite leads the brand’s new KEEN Made Originals collection dropping May 1 and it's built a little differently than what came before. Since moving from its homebase in Portland, Oregon, each pair is assembled at KEEN's new factory in Shepherdsville, Kentucky, using a heat-fused construction process that skips the glue entirely, a small but meaningful shift in how the shoe is put together and how long it holds up. Women's styles come in both a lace-up and a Mary Jane version, giving the classic shape a softer, style-forward reinterpretation. The Jasper KM Lite drops May 1 at the brand’s website and select retailers.
In partnership with KEEN.

posts.caption:
The quietest looks are always the loudest statements, and Avneet Kaur has known this for a while now. 🌸
Draped in the deep, unhurried cool of monochromatic indigo, she doesn’t chase the moment. She sets the pace for it. Denim-on-denim, lace against leather, coastal light and shadow, every element dialled into a frequency that’s entirely her own.
In collaboration with @aurelia_womenswear , this isn’t just a fashion story. It’s a portrait of a generation that leads with composure, one that finds its power not in the loudest room, but in the stillest one. ✨
The industry moves fast. Avneet doesn’t rush. And somehow, she’s always exactly where the moment needs her to be.
Cool, collected, and completely in control, this is the indigo hour. And it belongs to her.

@umathurman Chooses Happiness: For decades, Thurman has embodied a certain kind of unstoppable presence, and  this month, the icon returns to the grisly genre that cemented her stardom, as a bloodthirsty avenger in #PrettyLethal. But off-screen things are less grim: Thurman is energized by her work and looking forward to the possibilities of her next chapter—one where she’s making space for new roles, new interests, and more time for herself: “I think the choice to be happy is one that should be made regardless of any conditions.”

posts.caption:
Man of the Moment — Yashasvi Jaiswal (@yashasvijaiswal28) continues to rise, and we’re here for every second of it.
From dreaming of centuries in blue to turning those visions into reality and now hitting it out of the park as the opener for Rajasthan Royal (@rajasthanroyals) in IPL, his journey is the very definition of hard work meeting opportunity. Powered as much by an unshakable mindset as by his brilliance on the pitch, our cover star opens up about the drive, discipline, and hunger that fuel his remarkable ascent, “I don’t know where I’m going to go, but I want to enjoy the journey.”
Starting out as a cricketing prodigy, now 24, he’s all about keeping his head in the game, stopping every now and then, to savour the simple joys of life. And maybe that is the secret sauce to success, one game at a time.
Here’s raising a toast to the ‘player of the match’- Yashasvi Jaiswal!

posts.caption:
David Carson collaborates with The Macallan (@the_macallan) to refine its visual language across the Double Cask, Sherry Oak and Colour Collections. The redesign draws from the distillery’s architecture, with a sculpted bottle form and a triangular label referencing Spain’s Sherry Triangle. Colour takes on a narrative role, mapping oak influence and maturation across each collection. Typography gains presence, adding structure and clarity to the overall identity. The shift stays measured, anchored in the brand’s established character while updating its expression. A considered evolution where design, provenance, and craft move in sync.

Here’s the thing about a long weekend, it doesn’t ask you to do anything extraordinary, but still gives you just enough room to. And a really good table is always a good place to start.
Somewhere between the endless options, the decision fatigue, and the “honestly anywhere works,” we stopped choosing and started just showing up and the meal tasted exactly like that.
Chefs in 2026 are building menus that read like a point of view. The kind of dining where the catch-up goes on for three hours because nobody checked the time, where restaurant-hopping becomes the whole plot of the weekend.
These new tables are worth the reservation, the group chat debate, and getting dressed for.

posts.caption:
Introducing our latest cover star, @katehudson!
It’s been over 25 years since Kate Hudson earned her first Oscar nomination for her breakout role as Penny Lane in ‘Almost Famous,’ and she’s been shaping the cultural moment ever since. Despite her success—and her roots in Hollywood royalty—she’s remained remarkably grounded, continuing to speak about the industry with a genuine sense of gratitude.
“I just really want to make movies that people love,” Hudson tells L’OFFICIEL. “It’s really simple for me. I feel a responsibility as someone that loves this business, and grew up in it, and really wanted to make movies. I hope that I can make movies and shows that people really want to see.”
That, of course, has always been Hudson’s strength. Fresh off another Oscar nomination for her role in ‘Song Sung Blue,’ she’s now turning her attention back to television with Season 2 of ‘Running Point.’ In addition to executive producing the Netflix series, Hudson stars as Isla Gordon, a character loosely inspired by Los Angeles Lakers owner and president Jeanie Buss. While the role has prompted comparisons to Hudson’s own upbringing, she’s quick to dismiss that narrative. “Jeanie has had to maintain the most important team in sports history, and on top of that, the money and the franchise and everything that goes with it,” she says. “For me, we’re just actors. People are either gonna like what we do or they’re not.”
Read our full interview with Hudson about the collaborative nature of film, her creative process, and growing up in the industry at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
Nato a Hong Kong, formato a Londra e oggi protagonista del calendario dell’Haute Couture parigina — primo designer di origini hongkonghesi nella storia della Fédération —, @RobertWun è un creativo capace di trasformare ogni collezione in una narrazione visiva in tre atti, dove sogno, confronto e risveglio si susseguono con la logica inevitabile di un loop mentale. La sua ultima couture nasce da un gesto essenziale: riaprire il portfolio universitario, tornare al puro istinto di prima del rumore, prima delle aspettative. Il risultato è un racconto che oscilla tra bianco e nero assoluto e armature tempestose di micro pietre, tra la fragilità del sogno e il coraggio di proteggerlo. «La couture non deve riflettere ciò che siamo, ma ciò che desideriamo essere», dichiara. E in effetti il suo immaginario, che cita Miyazaki, McQueen e la grazia silenziosa come unica bussola, non conosce compromessi. Nel video ufficiale di Runway, uno dei pezzi chiave de Il diavolo veste Prada 2, Lady Gaga ne incarna tutta l’essenza indossando un abito couture della collezione primavera estate 2026. @cadeaudavril lo ha incontrato per L’OFFICIEL, leggi l’intervista al link in bio e sulle pagine del nostro ultimo numero, ora in edicola.
Photo: Robert Wun / Getty Images
#RobertWun #HauteCouture #LOfficielItalia

posts.caption:
Beyond a mere location, it is a narrative in motion. In the vibrant core of Milan, Salotto @Retori_official emerges: more than the debut flagship store born from @salmarachidm’s creative intuition, it is a sanctuary dedicated to connection and foresight. In this space, fashion transcends its form to become a cultural dialogue—a place where art and aesthetics intertwine to redefine a living, collective dimension. This is the new rhetoric of style.
Oltre lo spazio fisico, un racconto in divenire. Nel cuore pulsante di Milano, il Salotto @Retori_official si svela non solo come il primo flagship store frutto dell’intuizione di @salmarachidm, ma come un rifugio consacrato all’incontro e alla visione. Qui la moda supera il concetto di abito per farsi linguaggio culturale, in un ecosistema dove arte e bellezza coesistono per restituire una dimensione sociale e viva. Una nuova retorica del vestire.

posts.caption:
@KateHudson has taken on a wide range of genres, but she’s still focused on pushing herself even further. When we asked Hudson what she still hopes to achieve in her career, she didn’t point to accolades—just a desire to keep pursuing roles that scare her, the same approach she brings to everything she does. ⁠
⁠
“I enter every experience like it’s new, like I don’t know how it’s going to unfold,” she explains. “I come in doing my part, which is understanding what makes me feel confident in the role and the character, but I do that so that when I’m there, I can be flexible, not rigid.” Read Hudson’s full interview at the link in our bio. ⁠

posts.caption:
Introducing our latest cover star, @katehudson!
It’s been over 25 years since Kate Hudson earned her first Oscar nomination for her breakout role as Penny Lane in ‘Almost Famous,’ and she’s been shaping the cultural moment ever since. Despite her success—and her roots in Hollywood royalty—she’s remained remarkably grounded, continuing to speak about the industry with a genuine sense of gratitude.
“I just really want to make movies that people love,” Hudson tells L’OFFICIEL. “It’s really simple for me. I feel a responsibility as someone that loves this business, and grew up in it, and really wanted to make movies. I hope that I can make movies and shows that people really want to see.”
That, of course, has always been Hudson’s strength. Fresh off another Oscar nomination for her role in ‘Song Sung Blue,’ she’s now turning her attention back to television with Season 2 of ‘Running Point.’ In addition to executive producing the Netflix series, Hudson stars as Isla Gordon, a character loosely inspired by Los Angeles Lakers owner and president Jeanie Buss. While the role has prompted comparisons to Hudson’s own upbringing, she’s quick to dismiss that narrative. “Jeanie has had to maintain the most important team in sports history, and on top of that, the money and the franchise and everything that goes with it,” she says. “For me, we’re just actors. People are either gonna like what we do or they’re not.”
Read our full interview with Hudson about the collaborative nature of film, her creative process, and growing up in the industry at the link in bio.

In this space, fashion transcends its form to become a cultural dialogue—a place where art and aesthetics intertwine to redefine a living, collective dimension. This is the new rhetoric of style.

posts.caption:
Bridgerton isn’t just a romantic period drama, it’s one of Netflix’s most lavish productions. The series reportedly carries a staggering budget of around $168 million, placing it among the most expensive shows created by the streaming giant.
Each episode is estimated to cost around $7 million, funding the show’s extravagant costumes, grand sets, and cinematic storytelling that recreates Regency-era London. The scale of production is what gives the series its signature opulence and visual richness.
The spending doesn’t stop at sets and wardrobes. Reports also reveal impressive cast salaries, with some actors and voice talents earning up to $1 million for their roles, reflecting the show’s massive global success.
From lavish balls to royal romances, Bridgerton proves that luxury storytelling comes with a royal price tag and Netflix is willing to pay for it.
Images: Courtesy of Netflix

Elon Musk retains the top position, followed by Larry Page and Sergey Brin, with Jeff Bezos and Mark Zuckerberg also ranking among the top five. The list further includes Larry Ellison, Michael Dell, and Jensen Huang, alongside members of the Walmart founding family, Rob Walton and Jim Walton. ￼

posts.caption:
Prada’s cultural momentum saw a notable shift following the release of The Devil Wears Prada, a film that brought the inner workings of high fashion into mainstream conversation. While the brand was already established within luxury circles, the movie amplified its visibility, embedding Prada into global pop culture and redefining its relevance for a wider audience.
In the years that followed, Prada evolved beyond its cinematic association, refining its identity through intellectual design, sharp storytelling, and a balance between heritage and modernity. The brand’s rise has been shaped not by a single moment, but by its ability to consistently reinterpret fashion with a sense of purpose, aligning creativity with cultural awareness.
Today, Prada stands as a case study in sustained influence, where media exposure may have sparked broader recognition, but long-term growth has been driven by strategic reinvention, creative direction, and an unwavering commitment to innovation.
#Luxurious

#ManifestCover
From engineered gym run-ins to a romance that unfolded at its own pace, Allu Sirish (@allusirish) and Nayanika Reddy’s (@nayanika_reddy) story feels less like a whirlwind and more like a rhythm built over time.
“It wasn’t like we suddenly started going on back-to-back dates. It was more of a slow-burn romance,” says Sirish, recalling how a series of chance meetings — and one very intentional gym switch — finally brought them together.
For Nayanika, the turning point was simpler.
“I realised how easy it felt to be around him. Our conversations were natural, and there was always a sense of comfort. Over time, it became clear that we were building something meaningful,” she shares.
Just days before their wedding, the couple comes together for their first editorial shoot, speaking to Manifest about love that grew organically, families that were always one step ahead, and why the most meaningful relationships don’t need grand gestures to feel certain.
Our latest issue is now live on Magzter. Read the full cover story from the link in bio.

posts.caption:
#ManifestDigitalCover | The Shefali Shah Effect
For over three decades, Shefali Shah (@shefalishahofficial) has built a career on instinct, honesty, and an unwavering refusal to fit into boxes. From the unforgettable Pyari in Satya to the formidable DCP Vartika Chaturvedi in Delhi Crime, her performances have never relied on noise — only truth.
“A few years ago, if someone told me a woman in her mid-forties would lead a show, I would have laughed. And if they said it would be me, I would have laughed louder,” she says.
In our latest digital cover, the actor reflects on evolving with time, finding freedom in flawed characters, 25 years of marriage, and why success today means something far quieter than fame.

posts.caption:
#AJewelledSymphony
The Ektara has long been linked with the Bauls of Bengal, a group of wandering mystic minstrels. It has travelled from village to village as a medium of devotion and storytelling. Made from a dried coconut that is cut open, cleaned, and hollowed out. A wooden neck is then attached, and the hard outer shell becomes the body of the instrument. This shell acts as a natural sound chamber. Its hard, rounded surface reflects vibrations without absorbing them. When the string is plucked, the hollow space inside gathers the sound and sends it out with a bright, clear and slightly metallic tone. The shell is also relatively small, which means the sound it produces is sharp and focused rather than deep and booming. The musical note rises and fades quickly, giving the instrument its crisp and pulsing rhythm.
Necklace, Goenka India (@goenkaindia)
Styling: Palak Valecha (@_palakvalecha_)
Photographs: Adil Hasan (@tombomb_adil)
Fashion Assistants: Bhumi Jain (@bhumijnn) and Sumit Kumar (@sumitkumarr13)

Text: Mirza Shaina Beg (@mirzashainaa)
#Manifest #jewellery #Ektara

posts.caption:
#AJewelledSymphony
Native to Punjab, the tumbi is also known as toombi or tumba. It is closely tied with folk music and is a defining sound in Bhangra, the traditional folk dance of the region. Tumbi’s structure rests on a small dried gourd, which forms the resonating chamber. The gourd is cleaned and hollowed which creates a light but firm shell for amplifying vibrations. And the gourd’s compact size is what gives the instrument its bright, cutting sound. The wooden neck of the instrument is fixed into the gourd, and a single metal string is stretched across the body, passing over a small bridge, and tied to a tuning peg at the top. When the string is flicked back and forth with the index finger, the vibration travels into the hollow shell, where the gourd gathers and projects the sound, releasing a melodious sound. The clarity of the tumbi comes from this simplicity and is mostly used in devotional music.
Necklace, Sunita shekhawat (@sunita_shekhawat_jaipur)
Styling: Palak Valecha (@_palakvalecha_)
Photographs: Adil Hasan (@tombomb_adil)
Fashion Assistants: Bhumi Jain (@bhumijnn) and Sumit Kumar (@sumitkumarr13)
Sculpture piece: Stone Art (@stoneart.asia)

#Manifest #jewellery #tumbi

posts.caption:
#AJewelledSymphony
The Sitar draws from the legacy of the celestial veena of Saraswati, the goddess of music. Its long neck and resonant base gives it a rich, flowing sound that feels divine. Emerging in North India, sitar evolved into one of the central voices of Hindustani classical music, known for its meditative glide in between notes. At the heart of the instrument lies the tumba, the resonating chamber formed from a dried bottle gourd, which is turned into a hollowed musical vessel. The sitar can only be built from gourds of a particular size and curvature. They are harvested at full maturity to ensure the shell is thick enough to carry vibration without splintering under string tension. But the shells are not converted immediately after harvesting. Each gourd is hung to dry for months, allowing the inner pulp to loosen until the shell hardens to a stable density suitable for resonance. Once cured, it is cut open with the interior cleared of fibre and seeds. The gourd is then thinned and balanced by hand to ensure the quality of the sound. The dried gourd serves as a natural amplifier when it meets the vibrating strings of the sitar, generating a beautiful sound.
Necklaces and earrings, Hazoorilal Legacy Jewellers (@hazoorilallegacy)
Styling: Palak Valecha (@_palakvalecha_)
Photographs: Adil Hasan (@tombomb_adil)

posts.caption:
#AJewelledSymphony
Often associated with snake charmers and folk performers across Rajasthan and North India, the pungi is recognised by its sharp piercing sound that lingers. This wind instrument is made from a dried, hollowed gourd fitted with two bamboo pipes. The gourd forms the wind chamber which acts as a natural reservoir for air. Once it is cleaned and cured, the rounded interior of the gourd holds and regulates the air blown into it before it is channeled into the reed pipes of the instrument. A well-dried gourd, with firm and even walls, maintains steady air pressure, which is crucial for the uninterrupted low humming sound the pungi is known for.
Necklace, Amrapali Jewels (@amrapalijewels)

posts.caption:
For years, as the Met Gala has grown ever bigger, the questions and controversies surrounding New York’s “party of the year” have likewise proliferated.
Could the event, nominally a benefit for the Costume Institute of the Metropolitan Museum of Art, get any more high-profile? When most of the country was struggling, should any institution be charging $100,000 a ticket for a party? What would happen when Anna Wintour, the evening’s mastermind, retired?
The Costume Institute has been almost fully dependent on the gala as a source of its annual funds since the party began in 1948. Can it survive without the extravaganza?
It turns out the museum itself has been quietly working on an answer. Since 2016, the department has been putting some of the funds raised by the Met Gala into an endowment that, within a few years, should allow the department to support itself for the foreseeable future. What might that mean for the gala? Tap the link in our bio to read the full story. Photos by @danielterna, @vincenttullo

posts.caption:
On Tuesday, during its cruise show in Biarritz, France, Chanel introduced a creation that was not so much a shoe as a sh—. Not a sandal but a san—. Just small bits of leather cupping the model’s heels, held in place by angel-hair straps tied over the instep. The model’s feet, from about mid-arch to the toes, were left naked to feel the plush ivory carpet on which they walked. "I am sure they were thankful it was not a stark cement floor," the style reporter Jacob Gallagher wrote in his latest newsletter.
This design is gossamer to the point of becoming a metaphysical paradox. "But really, these heel caps represent irrational, nonsensical luxury at the highest tier — shoes made for feet that never touch the ground," @jacobwgallagher continued. At the link in our bio, read more about the design that is more foot than footwear, and subscribe to The Fashions newsletter. Photo by Stephane Cardinale/Corbis, via Getty Images

posts.caption:
The first Monday in May — a.k.a. the date of the Met Gala, a.k.a. the party of the year, a.k.a. the Oscars of the East Coast — is almost here. The clothes! The celebrities! The money! The chance to comment knowingly on all of the above while in your pajamas.
But this Met Gala is not just any old Met Gala. This Met Gala is celebrating both the next big Costume Institute show and the Costume Institute’s new home: the Condé M. Nast Galleries. Housed in the 11,500-square-foot space that used to be the Metropolitan Museum of Art’s gift shop, the fashion galleries will now be the first thing visitors see when they walk into the Great Hall. In other words: an undeniable declaration that fashion deserves a prime place in the most storied of fine arts institutions.
It’s a big deal, and may be why Beyoncé agreed to be a host for this year’s event, which will be her the first appearance on the Met carpet in a decade. Who else is hosting? Is there a theme? What’s the dress code? Everything you need to know about the 2026 Met Gala is in our guide. Tap the link in our bio to read it. Photos by @damonwinter, @landonnordeman, @kristaschlueter, @vnina
posts.alt_text: Photo by New York Times Fashion & Style on May 01, 2026. May be an image of magazine and text that says 'Your Guide to the 2026 2026MetGala Met Gala'.

posts.caption:
The cruise shows, those amorphous in-between collections that big brands have turned into traveling extravaganzas for very important clients and even more important social content, kicked off on Tuesday with Chanel.
The current hottest brand in the industry, according to the fashion search engine Lyst (which assesses brands according to multiple criteria including intent to buy, social conversation, speed of sell-through and cultural relevance), Chanel held its show in Biarritz, the French town where the house was founded in 1915. In the front row were the brand ambassadors ASAP Rocky and Nicole Kidman as well as Tilda Swinton, Sofia Coppola and Michaela Coel; watching onscreen was our fashion critic Vanessa Friedman. Even through the glow of her computer, certain elements stood out.
Blazy offered his spin on the little black dress, which turns 100 this year. Newsprint took on a new meaning. And there were ball gowns that seemed destined for next week’s Met Gala steps. Tap the link in our bio to read @vvfriedman's full review of @chanelofficial's cruise collection. Photos by Marc Piasecki/Getty Images, Chanel
